questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Transport (Roadways, Railways, Waterways, Ports, Airways, Pipelines, etc.)
questions.questionText:
A logistics company is preparing a report for a
foreign client who wants to route its ultra-large
container vessels through India. During the
briefing, the team highlights a particular Indian
port with the following characteristics:
I. 	 It can handle some of the world’s largest
vessels due to its deep draught and advanced
berthing facilities.
II. 	 It lies closest to major international shipping
routes compared to any other Indian port,
reducing transit time and fuel costs.
III. 	It is India’s only semi-automated port where
women operators control automated cranes
as part of a gender-inclusive operations
initiative.
Based on the information provided, the team is
most likely referring to which one of the following
ports?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Vizhinjam is India’s first dedicated deepwater international transhipment port.** It is located in Thiruvananthapuram, **Kerala**. Vizhinjam is **designed specifically to handle Ultra Large Container** **Ships (ULCS)** with minimal dredging. It can handle some of the world’s largest vessels due to its deep draught and advanced berthing facilities.
● One of Vizhinjam’s biggest strategic advantages is its proximity to the East-West international shipping route, which lies **just 10 nautical miles from the port**, **reducing transit time and fuel costs.** This also allows large container vessels to dock with minimal deviation, making it highly attractive for transhipment operations.
● This all-weather port is India’s first greenfield port project. As I**ndia’s first semi-automated port,** it also flaunts a skilled women workforce operating automated cranes, another first in India. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the second phase of development of the Vizhinjam International Seaport has begun.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Digital Life Certificate (DLC):
I. 	 It is an Aadhaar-based biometric service that
enables pensioners to submit their annual life
certificate digitally.
II. 	 It carries a legally valid digital signature
and is recognised under the Information
Technology Act, 2000.
III. 	It is not valid for a lifetime and must be
renewed every 5 years.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● The **Digital Life Certificate (DLC)**, or **Jeevan Pramaan**, is an **Aadhaar-based biometric system** that allows **pensioners to submit their annual life certificate digitally** through fingerprint, iris, or face authentication, without the need for physical presence. **So statement I is correct.**
● A DLC generated through the Jeevan Pramaan system carries a **legally valid digital signature** and is recognised under the provisions of the Information Technology Act, 2000. **So statement II is correct.**
● The purpose of the DLC system is precisely to **eliminate the requirement for pensioners** to appear physically before the Pension Disbursing Office. Once the DLC is generated, **physical verification is not** **required**.
● The Pramaan ID generated after submitting a Digital Life Certificate **does not remain valid for a lifetime**. Each DLC is valid only for the specific verification cycle mandated by the Pension Sanctioning Authority. As of now, pensioners have to **submit a Life Certificate every year** in the month of November for the continuation of their pension. **So statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the Department of Pension & Pensioner's Welfare conducted Nationwide Digital Life Certificate Campaign 4.0.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
International Conference Held at
I. - Mediterranean Raisina Dialogue, 2025
Brazil
II. - United Nations Ocean Conference, 2025
France
III. - Ramsar Conference of the Parties (COP15), 2025
Zambia
IV. - BRICS Summit, 2025 China
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Mediterranean Raisina Dialogue 2025 was held in Marseille, France**, as an extension of India’s flagship Raisina Dialogue. It focused on geopolitical convergence between Europe and the Indo-Pacific, maritime security, and supply chain resilience. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **The United Nations Ocean Conference, 2025 was held in Nice, France**, co-hosted by France and Costa Rica. The conference aimed to accelerate global action for ocean conservation and the blue economy by enhancing cooperation, financing, and marine scientific knowledge for better policymaking. **So, pair II** **is correctly matched.**
○ Outcome: The adoption of the “Nice Ocean Action Plan” that includes a political declaration and voluntary commitments to conserve and sustainably use the ocean” in support of Sustainable Development Goal 14 on life above and below the water.
● **The 15th Meeting of the Conference of the Contracting Parties (COP15), 2025 to the Ramsar** **Convention was at Victoria Falls in the Republic of Zimbabwe.** The key outcome was the adoption of 25 resolutions, including a review of the Fifth Strategic Plan of the Ramsar Convention. Promotion of wetlands as nature-based solutions, alignment with the Global Biodiversity Framework, climate resilience and Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **The 17th BRICS Summit was held in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.** The 17th BRICS summit theme: **“Strengthening Global South Cooperation and Promoting a More Inclusive and Sustainable Global** **Governance”**. The Key agenda included global health, trade & investment financing, AI governance and reform of global security architecture. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
○ The 25th Heads of State Council meeting of the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation (SCO) in Tianjin, China. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Ramsar Convention's 15th Conference of Parties (COP15) took place in Victoria Falls.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Deep Ocean Mission (DOM):
I. 	 It aims to develop technologies for deep-sea
mining and manned submersible to a depth of 6000 metres in the ocean.
II. 	 It will explore potential sites of multi-metal
hydrothermal sulphides mineralization along the Indian Ocean mid-oceanic ridges.
III.	 Samudrayan serves as India’s first human-
crewed deep-sea mission.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● Under the Deep Ocean Mission, a key focus is on **developing technology for deep‑sea mining and** **exploring polymetallic nodules** in the central Indian Ocean. The mission aims to work at **depths of about** **6000 metres**, where India is creating a crawler‑based mining system along with other subsea tools. **So** **statement I is correct**.
● The Mission also has a programme to improve India’s ocean‑based climate services. It focuses on building **advanced systems for ocean modelling, forecasting, and early warnings**. These tools will help coastal communities, boost disaster preparedness, and strengthen climate resilience along India’s shores.
● One of the components of the mission aims to **explore and identify potential sites of multi-metal** **hydrothermal sulphides mineralization along the Indian Ocean mid-oceanic ridges**. This component will additionally support the Blue Economy priority area of deep-sea exploration of ocean resources. **So** **statement II is correct.**
● **Samudrayan**, with the **MATSYA‑6000** submersible, is part of the Deep Ocean Mission. It is **India’s** **first project to send humans into deep‑sea environments** for scientific study, technology trials, and research at depths of up to 6000 metres. **So statement III is correct.**
● In August 2025, two Indian aquanauts successfully conducted deep ocean expeditions to depths of 4,025 meters and 5,002 meters in the Atlantic Ocean—marking the first time India has achieved such a feat. This placed the country among a select group of nations, including the USA, Russia, Japan, France and China, that have advanced underwater vehicles for subsea exploration. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently,  Indian aquanauts conducted a deep dive up to 5000 m in the Deep Sea and collected over 100 kg of cobalt-rich polymetallic nodules from the Andaman Sea.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Critical and Strategic Minerals (Lithium, Cobalt, Titanium, KABIL, REEs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the National Critical Mineral Mission (NCMM):
I. 	 The State Government holds exclusive
authority for auctioning leases of the critical minerals under the Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act, 1957.
II. 	 The Geological Survey of India (GSI) has
been assigned to identify and assess potential deposits of critical minerals.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● The **National Critical Mineral Mission (NCMM)** was announced in the Union Budget 2024–25 to strengthen India’s strategic autonomy in the mineral sector. It aims to secure India’s supply chain of critical minerals that are essential for clean energy technologies, electronics, defence, and advanced manufacturing.
● The Mission is implemented by the **Ministry of Mines**. This mission got **legislative backing by the 2023** amendment to the **Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act, 1957**.
● A committee formed by the Ministry of Mines in November 2022 identified 30 critical minerals, with 24 included in Part D of Schedule I of MMDR Act, 1957. The inclusion of 24 critical minerals in Part D of the First Schedule of the Act means that the **Central Government now has the exclusive authority to** **auction mining leases and composite licenses** for these specific minerals. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Geological Survey of India (GSI)** has been entrusted with conducting **large-scale exploration** **projects** to identify and assess potential deposits of critical minerals. The mission targets **1,200 domestic** **exploration projects** between FY 2024-25 and FY 2030-31. **So statement II is correct.**
● The Mission **supports technology development** in exploration, processing, and recycling. It **aims to file** **1,000 patents by 2030‑31** to build local capabilities and reduce dependence on foreign technology. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The government is pushing patents under NCMM and supporting innovation in critical minerals.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - She is popularly known as “the Philosopher Queen”.
II. - She established a textile industry in Maheshwar, famous for Maheshwari sarees.
III. She undertook renovation and repair of the
famous Kashi Vishwanath Temple.
IV. A National award is instituted in her memory
to honour the women who excel in the field of tribal and folk arts. Who was she?
Which of the following queens is being described above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ahilyabai Holkar** was the Queen of the Malwa kingdom. In the 18th century, as the Maharani of Malwa, she was instrumental in spreading the message of dharma and propagating industrialisation. She is widely known for her wisdom, courage, and administrative skills.
● Ahilyabai took control of Malwa after her husband's death in the battle of Kumher in 1754. She then laid the **foundation of the indomitable Holkar army in 1792** and became one of the most feared archers of her time.
● Ahilyabai also established a **textile industry in Maheshwar**, which today is very famous for its Maheshwari sarees.
● Her most notable contribution was the renovation and **repair of the famous Kashi Vishwanath Temple** **in 1780.** Recognising this contribution of hers, a statue of Devi Ahilyabai Holkar has been installed in the Kashi Vishwanath temple. Devi Ahilyabai is also credited with the construction of the Old (Juna) Somnath temple in Gujarat. Interestingly, this temple is popularly known as the Ahilyabai temple.
● She is known as **Punyashlok** (“One as Pure as the Sacred Chants”). John Keay, the British historian, gave the queen the title of **‘The Philosopher Queen’**.
● **National Devi Ahilyabai Award** has been instituted in memory of Ahilyabai Holkar to honor women who excel in the field of tribal, folk, and traditional arts.
● On May 31, 2025, on the occasion of the 300th birth anniversary of Ahilyabai Holkar, the government released a commemorative postage stamp and a special coin dedicated to Devi Ahilyabai. **The Rs 300** **coin** will feature a portrait of Ahilyabai Holkar. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, India celebrated the 300th birth anniversary of Lokmata Devi Ahilya Bai Holkar.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: What is AI Hallucination?

options.a:
An information that seems plausible but is
actually inaccurate
options.b:
A technique used to improve AI model
accuracy
options.c:
A training method where AI models learn by
simulating multiple decision pathways
options.d:
A diagnostic tool used to evaluate bias in
large language models

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Hallucinations occur when your sensory perception does not correspond to external stimuli.**  An **AI** **"hallucination"** refers to when an AI model **generates false, fabricated, or nonsensical information** **but presents it confidently as fact.**
● Engineers build AI systems by gathering massive amounts of data and feeding it into a computational system that detects patterns in the data. The system develops methods for responding to questions or performing tasks based on those patterns. When a system doesn't understand the question or the information that it is presented with, it may hallucinate.
● Many reports have indicated that some of the latest and most advanced AI models from companies like OpenAI (o3 and o4-mini, for example) have higher hallucination rates than their predecessors in certain tests. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Circular Error Probable (CEP):**
● A missile's error rate is measured using the **Circular Error Probable (CEP)**, which is the radius of a circle where 50% of impacts are expected to land.
**Relevance:** Recently, a technical report released by OpenAI found that the company’s latest models-o3 and o4mini, generate more errors than its older models. Computer scientists call the errors made by chatbots “hallucinations”.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following countries:
I. - Chad
II. - Ghana
III. Equatorial Guinea
IV. Niger
V. - Cameroon
How many of the above border Nigeria?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Nigeria** is an African country that is bordered to the east by **Cameroon** and **Chad** while the west is bound by **Benin**. **Niger** is to the north of Nigeria while the northeast is bordered by Lake Chad. The coast of the nation in the south lies along the **Gulf of** **Guinea**.
**So, only three of the above countries form a border with Nigeria.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The United States of America recently alleged the persecution of Christians in Nigeria and calling for military action against Islamist groups.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Fertiliser Type
I. - Urea Nitrogenous fertiliser
II. - Muriate of Potash Complex fertiliser
III. - Di-Ammonium Phosphate (DAP)
Micronutrient fertiliser
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Fertilisers** are concentrated plant nutrients made from inorganic chemicals. They are used to supply essential elements that plants need to grow well. Unlike organic manure, fertilizers contain nutrients in higher amounts and are applied in smaller quantities.
● Depending on their composition, Fertilisers are classified as sole Fertilisers, mixed Fertilisers, or those that contain micronutrients.
● **Sole fertilisers** are the fertilisers that supply only a single nutrient. Sometimes it may be accompanied by a minor element. Sole fertilisers are further grouped according to the nutrients they supply.
○ **Nitrogenous fertilisers:** These are prepared and applied as a source of nitrogen to the crop. Commonly available nitrogenous fertilisers are **urea**, ammonium sulphate, calcium ammonium nitrate, etc. **So, pair I is correctly matched.** ○ **Phosphorus or phosphatic fertilisers:** These fertilisers are the main source of phosphorus only. Some commercially available phosphatic fertilisers are single superphosphate, triple super phosphate, dicalcium phosphate. ○ **Potassic fertilisers:** These are applied as a source of potassium. Commonly used potassic fertilisers are **muriate of potash** and potassium sulphate. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● Fertiliser with a source of more than one macro-nutrient for the plant is known as **mixed fertiliser.** Commonly used mixed fertilisers are **di-ammonium phosphate** nitro-phosphate, ammonium phosphate, ammonium phosphate sulphate, calcium ammonium nitrate, etc. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
○ Almost all fertiliser companies are making soluble and field applicable mixed fertilisers. Now, NPK complex fertilisers are available in varying nutrient contents.
● **Fertiliser containing micronutrients:** These fertilisers supply elements needed in small amounts. These are of two types: Chelates chemical compounds and inorganic salts. **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India’s urea consumption is set to touch 40 million tonnes (mt) in the current fiscal.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
India has become the first country in the world to develop genome-edited rice varieties, ‘Kamala’ and ‘Pusa DST Rice 1’. In the above context,
which of the following are the potential benefits associated with these rice varieties?
I. - These promise a significant increase in yield.
II. - These promise to eliminate greenhouse gas emissions.
III. These will significantly reduce the use of
irrigation water.
IV. These exhibit improved tolerance to drought
and salinity.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Genome-edited rice** involves precise gene changes using CRISPR-Cas9 technology, improving traits without introducing foreign DNA.
● Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) has developed the **world’s first genome-edited rice** **varieties**—**DRR Dhan 100** (**Kamala**) and **Pusa DST Rice 1**, aimed at **enhancing yields, climate** **resilience, and resource efficiency** without introducing foreign DNA. **So, statements I, II are correct.**
● **The DRR Rice 100 (Kamala):** Its objective is to **increase the number of grains** per panicle and it matures 20 days earlier (~130 days). Due to its shorter duration, it helps **save water** and fertilizers, **drought tolerance** and **reduces methane gas emissions.** Its stalk is strong and does not fall. The rice quality is similar to the original variety, Samba Mahsuri. **So. statement III is not correct.**
○ Kamala’s shorter maturity period allows for **fewer irrigations** which will lead to the saving of 7,500 million cubic metres of irrigation water, which can benefit other crops.
● The second variety, Pusa DST Rice 1, **can increase yields by 9.66% to 30.4%** in saline and alkaline soils, with the potential for up to **20% increase in production. It is drought and salinity tolerant. So,** **point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, ICAR unveiled the first genome-edited rice with high yield, drought and salinity tolerance, boosting climate resilience and water savings.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Operation Related to
I. - Operation Kagar
Counter-insurgency mission by India
II. - Operation Secure
National cybersecurity drive led by India
III. - Operation Sindhu
Evacuation of Indian Nationals from Iran
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● **Operation Kagar:** It is a **counter-insurgency mission** launched by the Union Ministry of Home Affairs. It primarily targets the dense forest regions of Chhattisgarh and Telangana. It is led by the Central Reserve Police Force (CRPF), along with state forces such as the District Reserve Guards and Greyhounds. This mission aligns with the central government’s goal of eliminating Naxalism in India, with a target to achieve this by March 31, 2026. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Operation Secure**: It is a regional **cybersecurity initiative** carried out under the Asia and South Pacific Joint Operations Against Cybercrime Project. It is **led by INTERPOL** through the ASPJOC framework. It involved **countries** across the Asia-Pacific region, **including India**. So it is **not a country specific** **initiative**. The operation focused on taking down malware networks by dismantling command and control servers. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Operation Sindhu**: It is an **evacuation mission** launched by the Government of India in 2025 **to bring** **back Indian nationals from Iran** during the rising conflict between Iran and Israel. **So, pair III is** **correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: To safeguard its citizens amid the Israel–Iran conflict, India launched Operation Sindhu to evacuate nationals from Iran.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Which of the following countries shares a land border with Croatia?
I. - Slovenia
II. - Hungary
III. Serbia
IV. Italy
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● Croatia is located in **Southeastern Europe.** It lies **between Central Europe and the Mediterranean** **region.** It shares its borders with several countries. It shares land borders with **Slovenia** to the northwest, **Hungary** to the northeast, **Serbia** to the east, **Bosnia** **and Herzegovina** to the southeast, and **Montenegro** to the south. It shares a **maritime border with Italy** across the **Adriatic Sea**. **So, points I, II and III are correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the Prime Minister of India visited Cyprus and Croatia on his way to Canada to participate in the G-7 summit.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Military Exercise Countries Involved
I. - Nomadic Elephant,
India and Australia
II. - Khaan Quest, 2025 Mongolia and United States
III. - Talisman Sabre, 2025 India and Mongolia
Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Nomadic Elephant** is a bilateral military exercise between **India and Mongolia**. It is conducted alternately in both countries and focuses primarily on **counter-insurgency and counter-terrorism** **operations** in mountainous terrain. The exercise enhances interoperability between the two armies and strengthens defence cooperation.  **So, pair 1 is not correctly matched.**
● **Khaan Quest began** in 2003 as a joint event **between the United States and Mongolian Armed Forces**. In 2006, it expanded into a **multinational peacekeeping exercise**. **India** is one of the participating countries to the exercise in 2025. The goal is to improve peacekeeping skills under the UN mandate. It also helps nations share best practices in peacekeeping, humanitarian aid, and civil-military coordination. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Exercise Talisman Sabre** is a major training event **led by the Australian Defence Force and the United** **States Armed Forces**. In 2025, it involves **representation from 19 nations,** including Canada, Fiji, France, Germany, **India,** Indonesia, Japan, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Papua New Guinea, the Philippines, South Korea, Singapore, Thailand, Tonga, and the United Kingdom. **So, pair III is not** **correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: India, along with other nations, participated in Talisman Sabre 2025.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: Cathepsin B is primarily known as:

options.a:
A lysosomal enzyme involved in protein
degradation
options.c: A structural protein in connective tissues
options.d:
A neurotransmitter released at neuromuscular
junctions

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● **Cathepsin B** is a cysteine protease (lysosomal enzyme) secreted by the rough endoplasmic reticulum that can cross the blood-brain barrier. It is generally **involved in protein degradation**, while extracellularly it exists in both soluble and membrane-bound forms and participates in extracellular matrix protein degradation. It has also been found in secretory granules and is involved in prohormone processing.
● Recently, the scientific team, using both live mouse models and cultured goat ovaries, found that reducing the activity of a cellular protein called ‘Cathepsin B’ (Cat B) helps preserve the ovarian reserve. This ovarian reserve is the finite pool of egg cells (oocytes) that female mammals are born with. Unlike sperm, these crucial egg cells cannot be regenerated. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, researchers at the National Institute of Animal Biotechnology (NIAB) uncovered a molecular clue to slow down reproductive ageing.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 The International Civil Aviation Organisation
(ICAO)
II. 	 The International Air Transport Association
(IATA)
III. 	Airports Council International (ACI)
Which of the following statements about these organisations is **not** correct?

options.a:
ICAO is a UN-affiliated regulatory agency
for global aviation.
options.b:
IATA operates as an arm of ICAO to
implement aviation regulations.
options.c:
ACI is a global association of airport
operators.
options.d:
All three have their headquarters in Montreal,
Canada.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● The **International Civil Aviation Organisation (ICAO)** is a **UN specialised agency**. It **sets global** **Standards and Recommended Practices (SARPs)** for aviation safety, security, efficiency, and environmental protection. **So option (a) is correct.**
● The **International Air Transport Association (IATA)** is a **trade association of the world’s airlines**, representing around 300 airlines. It focuses on aviation safety, ticketing, and operational coordination. **So** **option (b) is not correct.**
● **Airports Council International (ACI)** is the **global association of airport operators**. It represents airports in areas of safety, security, and airport management. **So option (c) is correct.**
● ICAO, IATA and ACI all three have their headquarters in **Montreal, Canada.  So option (d) is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the central government told the Supreme Court that the Air India Ahmedabad crash probe follows the International Civil Aviation Organisation standards.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
What makes Short Tandem Repeats (STRs) ideal
for forensic identification in DNA profiling?

options.a:
They differ in how many times the DNA unit
repeats at the same genomic site.
options.b:
They are located within coding regions of
DNA.
options.c: They consist of highly conserved sequences.
options.d:
They produce repeat patterns that remain
uniform.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **DNA profiling** is a technique used to identify individuals based on variations in their DNA. It compares specific regions of the genome that differ significantly among people. These differences help establish identity in forensics, paternity testing, missing person identification, and disaster victim analysis.
● Short Tandem Repeats (STRs) are short repeating sequences of 2–6 base pairs scattered throughout the human genome. They **occur mostly in non-coding regions** and **exhibit high variability (non-conserved** **sequences)**, meaning different individuals often have **different numbers of repeat units at the same** **genomic location, meaning they vary in repeat numbers at the same genomic site**. This makes STRs ideal for forensic identification. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The victims of the Ahmedabad AI crash were identified through DNA profiling.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Pollution Related (E-Waste, Solid Waste, Pollutants, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Ballast Water Management Convention, 2004
II. 	 Hazardous and Noxious Substances (HNS)
Convention, 2010
III. 	Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) Convention,
IV. 	 International Convention for the Prevention of
Pollution from Ships (MARPOL) Convention,
How many of the above are ratified by India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Ballast Water Management Convention (BWM) was adopted by the **International Maritime** **Organization (IMO)** in 2004. It **aims** to **prevent the spread of harmful aquatic organisms through** **ships’ ballast water**. It requires ships to install ballast water treatment systems and meet discharge standards to protect aquatic ecosystems. **India** has **not ratified this** Convention. **So, point I is not correct.**
● The HNS Convention, 2010 establishes a **liability and compensation regime** for damage **caused by the** **maritime transport of hazardous and noxious substances** (chemicals, oils, gases). It supplements the oil-pollution liability regime and aims to ensure prompt compensation to victims. **India has not ratified** **the HNS Convention**. **So, point II is not correct.**
● The **SOLAS Convention (Safety of Life at Sea)** is the most important global treaty on ship safety. It lays down **mandatory standards on ship construction**, fire protection, life-saving appliances, navigation safety, communication systems, and safety management. **India** has **ratified** **SOLAS**. **So, point III is** **correct.**
● **The MARPOL (International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships) is the main** **international treaty to prevent pollution from ships.** It deals with oil spills, chemicals, harmful substances in packages, sewage, garbage, and air emissions. **India** has **ratified MARPOL**. **So, point IV** **is correct.** **So, only two of the given points are correct.** **Relevance**: Recently, the Liberian‑flagged container ship MSC ELSA‑3 sank off the coast of Kochi, Kerala, raising questions about international maritime conventions and safety obligations.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** An air conditioner (AC) becomes less energyefficient when it cools a room to very low temperatures.
**Statement II:** The greater the temperature difference between the room air and the AC's set temperature, the lesser is the heat-transfer efficiency of the AC.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The energy efficiency of an air conditioner (AC) indicates how effectively it cools a room while using the minimum amount of electricity. When the AC is set to very low temperatures, its efficiency drops because **the compressor must run longer and work harder, using more electricity**. Even lowering the set temperature by a few degrees results in a sharp increase in power consumption. According to the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), increasing the AC’s set temperature by 1 °C can save about 6% energy.  **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● When the AC is set to a much lower temperature than the room, **the temperature gap widens**. A larger gap forces the **cooling cycle to operate at a lower evaporator temperature and a higher compressor** **load**. This reduces the system’s coefficient of performance (COP). In short, the greater the difference, the harder the AC works and the less efficient the heat-transfer process becomes. **So, statement II is correct**
● The reduction in efficiency at very low set temperatures happens **because** of the inefficiency in heat transfer due to a large temperature differential. So, both Statement I and Statement-II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recent guidelines published by the power ministry limit the operational temperature range of air conditioners across the country.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are examples of soft
matter?
I. 	 Toothpaste
II. 	 Soap bubbles
III. 	Amorphous silicon
IV. 	 Living cells
V. 	 Jellies
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **d** **Explanation**:
● **Soft matter** is any material which has **low elastic moduli**, meaning they deform easily under small stresses, and their behavior lies **between that of liquids and solids**. Their internal energy scales are comparable to **thermal energy at room temperature**, making them highly responsive to small forces.
● **Toothpaste** is a **colloidal gel or paste**, where solid particles are dispersed in a fluid medium, and colloids and gels are classic examples of soft matter due to their **viscoelastic behaviour** and sensitivity to small stresses. So, it is an example of soft matter.  **So, point I is correct.**
● **Soap bubbles** consist of thin liquid films with trapped air, making them **highly deformable** and governed by surface forces rather than rigid bonds. **So, these are** examples of soft matter.  **So, point II is correct.**
● **Amorphous silicon**, despite being non-crystalline, is still a rigid solid with applications ranging from photovoltaics and thin-film transistors to electrodes in batteries. It does not behave like gels, colloids or biological materials, so it is not soft matter. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Living cells** are composed of membranes, proteins and cytoskeletal networks. They exhibit **viscoelasticity**, **thermal fluctuation sensitivity**, and **soft deformability.** So it comes within the category of soft matter. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Jellies** are hydrogels, a key category of soft matter widely discussed in soft-matter physics literature. They consist of polymer networks swollen by water, giving them soft, elastic properties. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Researchers are developing interest in soft matter because small interactions create surprising everyday behaviours.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
The duration of a single second is currently
defined by:

options.b: Vibration frequency of a quartz crystal
options.d: Orbital period of the Earth around the Sun

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **a** **Explanation**:
● **One second is defined using the Caesium‑133 atom.** When this atom shifts between two energy levels, it gives off radiation at a very stable frequency. One second is the time it takes for **9,192,631,770 cycles** of that radiation. This definition is maintained by the **International Bureau of Weights and Measures** **(BIPM)**. Because of this, **caesium atomic clocks** are the main standard for measuring time.
● While newer **optical atomic clocks** (e.g., based on strontium, ytterbium, or trapped ions) are far **more** **accurate than caesium clocks** and are being considered for redefining the second, they are **not yet used** in the official definition.
● **Quartz crystals** and **Earth’s orbital period** are **not used in the current SI definition** of the second. Quartz oscillators are used in many clocks, but these are secondary and not fundamental definitions. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Global researchers have finished the largest clock comparison to prepare for redefining the second.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Missile Developed by
I. - Fattah Iran
II. - Javelin France
III. - Iskander-M Russia
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Fattah, meaning “Conqueror” in Persian, is Iran’s first domestically designed hypersonic** **medium-range ballistic missile**. Developed by the IRGC Aerospace Force it features solid-fuel propulsion and a two-stage setup. It has a range of approximately 1,400 km and terminal speeds between Mach 13 and Mach 15, allowing it to strike targets across the Middle East, including Israel. **So, pair I is** **correctly matched.**
● **Javelin is a highly advanced American-made anti-tank guided missile system,** developed by Raytheon and Lockheed Martin. The man-portable, shoulder-fired, anti-armour system automatically guides itself to the target after launch, allowing the gunner to take cover and avoid counterfire. The Javelin, which has been used in Afghanistan and Iraq, can be deployed from multiple platforms and used during the day, at night, and in all weather conditions. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **The Iskander (SS-26 Stone) is a road-mobile short-range ballistic missile system developed by** **Russia**. It entered Russian service in 2006, and it was first used by the Russians in 2008 against Georgia. It has a maximum range of 500 km and carries payloads between 480 and 700 kg, and is designed for tactical strikes on small, high-value land targets. **So, pair III is correctly matched**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India has submitted a letter of request to the United States for co-production of Javelin anti-tank guided missiles (ATGMs) in the country.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
In India, the Right to vote in the Lok Sabha and
State Legislative Assembly elections is a:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**:
● **Article 326 of the Constitution of India grants every citizen the right to vote, without any** **discrimination**. It provides that every citizen who is at least 18 years of age on such date as may be fixed by law and is not otherwise disqualified under the Constitution or any law on specific grounds shall be entitled to be registered as a voter.
● The **right to vote** is mentioned under Article 326 in the Constitution of India and according to the Supreme Court, any law or right mentioned in the constitution, not under any specific category is **known as** **Constitutional Law or Right.** Since the right to Vote is mentioned under the Constitution and not under the category of Fundamental Rights, it is said to be a Constitutional Right.
● **Statutory or legal rights are provided and amended by ordinary laws of Parliament or the State** **legislature**. Like, the right to work under the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee (MGNREGA) Act 2005, the rights of scheduled tribes under the Forest Rights Act 2006, and the right to subsidised food grains under the National Food Security Act 2013, among others. These are enforceable according to the legal process outlined in the laws that provide these rights.
● **Natural Rights** imply that every person, simply by virtue of being born as a human being, is entitled to certain basic rights. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The Supreme Court, while hearing challenges to Bihar’s Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls, has taken up the issue of the legal status of the right to vote.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the World Health Assembly:
I. 	 It is responsible for appointing the WHO
Director-General and overseeing the organisation’s financial policies.
II. 	 It functions as an executive body that
implements WHO programmes and enforces health regulations across member states.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● The **World Health Assembly** is the **decision-making body** of the **World Health Organisation (WHO)**, the United Nations (UN) agency **dedicated to promoting the health of the global population** and access to the highest levels of healthcare provision. Delegates from WHO member states gather at an annual assembly held at the UN headquarters in Geneva, Switzerland, to focus on a specific healthcare agenda developed by the organisation’s Executive Board**.** **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **The functions of the assembly include voting to appoint the WHO Director-General to serve a five-** **year term** and overseeing financial policies, **approving the proposed budget programme to fund** **future WHO operations**, monitoring progress in implementing work programmes and devising strategies to address gaps. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Health Assembly also serves as a forum for reporting back on the implementation of the areas of work set, in order to determine what has been achieved and decide on strategies for addressing the gaps. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the 78th World Health Assembly (WHA) unanimously adopted a resolution titled ‘Skin diseases as a global public health priority.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Peru has a greater share than Chile in India’s
copper import basket.
II. 	 Indonesia is the top exporter of copper ore to
India.
III. 	Panama contributes less than 10 per cent to
India’s copper imports.
IV. 	 Collectively, Peru, Chile, Indonesia and
Panama contribute 75 per cent to India’s copper import. All of the above statements are correct except:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● Despite having significant copper ore deposits in the country, India is still reliant on imports to meet its demand.  Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh and Jharkhand dominate in copper ore reserves of India. **In 2023,** India imported 1 million tonnes of copper concentrate**,** with Chile accounting for 25% and Peru for **14%.** **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Indonesia is the top exporter of copper to India,** accounting for about 27% of India's copper ore and concentrate imports**.** Indonesia, with an estimated 21 million metric tons of copper reserves, is the world's seventh-largest copper producer**. So, statement II is correct.**
● **Panama contributes 9%** to India’s copper ore and concentrates imports**. Cobre Panamá** is one of the largest copper mines globally and has recently been shut down, affecting Panama's copper production**. So,** **statement III is correct.**
● The global copper landscape is highly concentrated, with a few geographies dominating its extraction, and China owns over 44% of the global processing capacity. **Collectively, Peru, Chile, Indonesia and** **Panama are responsible for around 75% of India's copper concentrate imports.**
● Australia is another important source of copper exports to India, along with the USA, UAE, and Germany,
which constitute the bulk of copper scrap exports to India**. So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Copper mining landscape in India:**
● Historically, companies such as Hindustan Copper Limited and Hindalco Industries have been key players in the copper sector, with refineries located in Ghatsila, Jharkhand, and Dahej, Gujarat, respectively.
● **Vedanta's Sterlite** Copper plant in Thoothukudi, Tamil Nadu, produced 40 per cent of India’s copper till its closure in 2018 due to environmental concerns. This led to a significant reduction in domestic refining capacity, turning India into a net importer of refined copper.
● To mitigate copper shortage, the Indian government has secured exploration rights for greenfield land in Zambia, which it hopes to convert into mining rights.
● Additionally, the Ministry of Mines has introduced a new mineral concession, known as an **Exploration Licence**, for reconnaissance and prospecting operations related to critical and deep-seated minerals.
**Relevance**: The United States' imposition of a 50% tariff on copper imports to boost US copper production and the subsequent impact on India’s copper export to the United States.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Which of the following countries are part of the
Eurozone?
I. 	 Germany
II. 	 France
III. 	Ukraine
IV. 	 Croatia
V. 	 Denmark
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.a: I, II, III and V only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● The **eurozone,** officially known as the euro area, is a geographic and economic region that consists of all the European Union (EU) countries that have fully incorporated the euro as their national currency.
● EU states that want to join the single currency area must demonstrate that their economy has converged with other eurozone countries and that they are focused on sound public spending. The conditions include holding inflation to no more than 1.5% points higher than the rate of the three best-performing EU countries.
● **Germany is the most populous country in the eurozone, and France is the largest by surface area.** **So, points I and II are correct.**
● **Ukraine is not part of the Eurozone. Ukraine has had official EU candidate status** with accession negotiations ongoing**. So, point III is not correct.**
● **Denmark joined the European Union in 1973, but it has negotiated an opt-out from the euro** and is thus not obliged to introduce it**. So, point V is not correct.**
● **Croatia adopted the Euro in 2023,** making it the 20th member of the Eurozone**. So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The European Union mandates a set of standards known as convergence criteria that member states must fulfil to adopt the euro as their currency. Also referred to as the **Maastricht criteria**, the standards were established in the Maastricht Treaty of 1992.
● The convergence criteria consist of set benchmarks around four economic principles: **inflation, public** **finances, long-term interest rates and currency exchange rates.**
● The benefits of adopting the Euro include the elimination of exchange rate risks as well as price transparency and lower cross-border transaction costs, which can boost international trade and investment. Adopting the euro can also help improve borrowing conditions, as eurozone countries can access lower interest rates on sovereign debt, given their economies are backed by the European Central Bank, which dictates monetary policy across the bloc.
● **Currently, the euro is the world’s second most popular reserve currency, behind the US dollar**. It is used in almost 40% of cross-border payments worldwide and in nearly half of all EU exports.
**Relevance:** Bulgaria officially approved to adopt the euro on January 1, 2026, becoming the 21st member of the single currency area.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Tribe State/Union Territory
I. - Kuki Manipur
II. - Jarawa Andaman & Nicobar Islands
III. - Hmar Mizoram
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Kuki (Kuki–Zo) people are mainly a group of hill tribes who live mostly in Manipur**, Mizoram, Nagaland, Assam, and across the border in Myanmar. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **The Jarawas are an indigenous, Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group (Austro-Negrito) tribe of the** **Andaman Islands.** They inhabit the interior forests of the Middle and North Andaman Islands. **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.**
● **The Hmar is a tribal community mainly found in Mizoram**, and in smaller numbers in neighbouring states such as Manipur, Assam and Meghalaya. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Manipur-based organisations have urged the Ministry of Tribal Affairs to delete the 'Any Kuki Tribes' category from the Scheduled Tribes (ST) list in the State.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Climate Risk Index
II. 	 State of the Climate in Asia report
III. 	Air Quality and Climate Bulletin
IV. 	 Emissions Gap Report
How many of the above reports/indices are released by the World Meteorological Organisation (WMO)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **World Meteorological Organisation (WMO)** is a specialised agency of the United Nations responsible for the state and behaviour of the Earth's atmosphere, its interaction with oceans and land, and the resulting water resources.
● **Reports released by the World Meteorological Organisation (WMO) are:** ○ **State of the Climate in Asia report:** It is released by the WMO. The 2024 report analyses the climate trends, extreme events, temperature changes, glacier loss, rainfall anomalies, and hydrological impacts in Asia. It highlights how the region is warming faster than the global average. The report includes socio-economic impacts such as displacement, food security concerns, and disaster losses. **So, point II is correct.**
○ **Air Quality and Climate Bulletin:** This bulletin examines the interaction between **air pollution** **and climate change**, showing how both issues are linked. It presents global data on greenhouse gases, aerosols, particulate matter, and air quality trends. The report shows how climate change worsens air pollution events such as wildfires and heatwaves. It also tracks atmospheric composition and pollutants that have both climate and health impacts. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Climate Risk Index (CRI):** It is released by Germany-based NGO **Germanwatch**. The CRI assesses the level of exposure and vulnerability that countries face to extreme weather events. The index highlights
which countries are most affected by climate impacts in a given year and over long-term periods. **So, point** **I is not correct.**
● **Emissions Gap Report:** It is released annually by the United Nations Environment Programme (**UNEP)**
which assesses the difference between the world’s current projected greenhouse gas emissions and the levels needed to meet the Paris Agreement targets. It evaluates national climate pledges (NDCs), analyses whether countries are on track to limit warming to 1.5°C or 2°C, and highlights sector-wise mitigation opportunities and policy pathways. **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** According to the World Meteorological Organisation’s (WMO) State of the Climate in Asia 2024 report the year 2024 was the warmest year on record in Asia.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation (SCO):
I. 	 China, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan,
Tajikistan, and India are founding member States of the SCO.
II. 	 All the full members of the SCO, including
India, endorsed the Tianjin Declaration, 2025.
III. 	SCO member countries represent nearly 40%
of the global population and around 25% of the global GDP.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO)** was established in2001. It evolved from the earlier Shanghai Five grouping (1996), which consisted of **China, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and** **Tajikistan**. The SCO was created to foster regional cooperation, address common security threats such as terrorism, separatism, and extremism, and strengthen mutual trust among neighbouring states.
● **Its founding members were China, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan.** In 2017, India and Pakistan joined the SCO as full members. In 2023, Iran became a member, followed by Belarus in 2024, bringing the total number of SCO member states to **10**. The SCO also has two observer states—Afghanistan and Mongolia—and 14 dialogue partners. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The 2025 SCO Summit was hosted in Tianjin, China. The **Tianjin Declaration was signed and adopted** **by all members** including **India**, Pakistan, and Russia at 2025 SCO Summit The declaration reaffirmed the bloc’s “firm commitment to fight against terrorism, separatism, and extremism”, and stressed the inadmissibility of attempts to use terrorist, separatist, and extremist groups for “mercenary purposes” **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The combined population of **th**e **SCO member countries accounts for over 42.8% of the global** **population**, representing approximately 3.4 billion out of 8 billion people worldwide. The SCO countries contribute **23.6% of global nominal GDP** and around **33.8% of global GDP on a Purchasing Power** **Parity** (PPP) basis, making it one of the largest regional organisations in terms of demographic and economic weight. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Indian Prime Minister visited Tianjin, China, to participate in the 25th SCO Summit 2025.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Sustainable Development
Goals (SDG) Index, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is released annually by the UN Sustainable
Development Solutions Network.
II. 	 India ranks lower than all other BRICS
nations on the latest SDG Index.
III. 	In the latest edition, India ranked 99th out of
167 countries, marking its first time among the top 100.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) Index** is published every year by the UN Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN). SDSN works under the auspices of the UN Secretary-General. It ranks all UN member states on their performance toward achieving the 17 SDGs. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **In the 2025 edition, India ranked 99th out of 167 countries, marking its first time among the top 100** with a score of 67 on the SDG Index. South Africa ranked 111th with a score of 64.11. India does rank relatively low compared to many emerging economies, but **does not rank lower than all other BRICS** **nations. South Africa has ranked lower than India** in the 2025 edition of the SDG Index. **So, statement** **II is not correct and III is correct.**
○ China is ranked 49th with a score of 74.4, while the United States stands at 44th with 75.2 points.
**Knowledge box**
● The SDG Index measures a country’s progress across all 17 goals and 169 targets using datasets covering:
○ poverty & hunger, ○ health, education, gender equality, ○ climate action, biodiversity, energy, water, ○ peace, governance, and partnerships.
● Indicators include both global datasets and nationally sourced data.
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, India ranked 99th out of 167 countries in the recent Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) Index.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Financial Action Task Force
(FATF), consider the following statements:
I. 	 It was established at the G7 Summit, 1989 to
develop policies to combat money laundering.
II. 	 It makes decisions by consensus, and no
formal voting system exists.
III. 	Both India and Pakistan are members of
FATF.
IV. 	 South Africa and Nigeria were removed from
the FATF Grey List in 2025.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Financial Action Task Force (FATF) was established in 1989 at the **G7 Summit in Paris.** Its original mandate was to develop policies to **combat money laundering.** Over time, its scope expanded to include counter-terrorist financing (2001) and countering the financing of proliferation of weapons of mass destruction (2012). **So, statement I is correct.**
● The FATF functions through **consensus-based decision-making,** meaning it has **no formal voting** **system.** All key actions such as adding or removing countries from its lists require unanimous agreement among members. This makes the FATF a unique intergovernmental body where cooperation and collective consensus are essential for all decisions. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **India has been a full FATF member since 2010.** **Pakistan, however, is not a full FATF member**; it belongs to the Asia/Pacific Group on Money Laundering (APG), an FATF-style regional body. Although Pakistan was placed on and later removed from the FATF grey list (most recently delisted in October 2022). **So, statement III is not correct.**
● Recently, in October 2025, **South Africa and Nigeria were also removed from the FATF grey list** after demonstrating significant improvements in their anti-money laundering and counter-terror financing frameworks. Currently, North Korea, Iran, and Myanmar are on the FATF’s blacklist. **So, statement IV** **is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In June 2025, the Financial Action Task Force (FATF), stated that the Pahalgam terror “could not occur without money and the means to move funds between terrorist supporters”.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
The S-400 Triumf system belongs to which of the
following categories of air defence?

options.c:
Close-in weapon system designed for point
defence of a naval ship

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **S-400 Triumf** is an **advanced long-range Surface-to-Air Missile (SAM)** system developed by **Russia’s Almaz-Antey.** It is designed to deliver layered, high-precision air defence against a wide spectrum of **aerial threats.** It is capable of neutralising fighter aircraft, UAVs, cruise and ballistic missiles and even stealth platforms. The system offers exceptional detection and interception capabilities.
● It can track and engage up to **80 targets simultaneously** and employs a flexible engagement envelope, striking everything from low-flying drones to high-speed, high-altitude missiles.
● With a detection range of **nearly 600 km** and an engagement **range of up to 400 km,** the S-400 uses **four** **specialised missile** types to optimise performance across short, medium and long distances.
● Its interceptors reach speeds of **Mach 4.8**, enabling it to destroy targets travelling at more than **1,000 m/s,** making it one of the world’s most formidable air defence systems.
● In Indian service, the S-400 has been officially named **“Sudarshan Chakra”**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Russia has assured India that the delivery of the S-400 air defence system will proceed on schedule.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Trachoma
II. 	 Lymphatic Filariasis
III. 	Visceral leishmaniasis
IV. 	 Smallpox
How many of the above diseases have been eliminated from India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Trachoma:** It is a bacterial infection of the eyes caused by **Chlamydia trachomatis**. It’s a leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide, particularly in impoverished areas with poor access to clean water and sanitation. The **World Health Organisation (WHO) has declared India free of Trachoma. So,** **point I is correct.**
● **Lymphatic Filariasis:** The definition of Elimination of Lymphatic Filariasis (LF) is to achieve **<1%** **microfilaria rate (Mf rate)** in the endemic areas. The global target for Lymphatic Filariasis is the elimination of the disease as a public health problem by 2030. **India has not yet declared itself free from** **Lymphatic Filariasis (LF).** India remains steadfast in its commitment to eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis by 2027. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Visceral leishmaniasis (Kala-azar):** The definition for kala-azar elimination as a public health problem is to achieve an annual **incidence of less than one case per 10,000 population** at block level. Once achieved, the elimination is to be sustained for 3 years for KA elimination certification. India’s National Health Policy (2002) initially set a target of eliminating Kala-azar by 2010, but this was later revised to 2015, 2017, and then 2020. The WHO’s Neglected Tropical Disease roadmap set a goal of eliminating
Kala-azar by 2020, but the target was not achieved. **The WHO is now accelerating work to achieve the** **target by 2030.** **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Smallpox: India** was declared **free of smallpox in 1977**, with the last case detected in 1977, following intensified vaccination campaigns led by the World Health Organization (WHO) as part of a global effort that certified smallpox eradicated worldwide in 1980. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, only two of the above diseases have been declared eliminated or eradicated from India.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Prime Minister of India has said that India has been declared free of Trachoma, an eye disease.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Kishanganga and Ratle hydroelectric projects:
I. 	 Both projects are located in the Union
Territory of Jammu and Kashmir.
II. 	 Both are designed as run-of-the-river
hydroelectric projects.
III. 	Both are situated on tributaries of the Jhelum
river system.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Ratle Hydroelectric Project** is an 850 MW **run-of-the-river power plant** being constructed in the Kishtwar district, **Jammu & Kashmir.** It is being built on the **Chenab River**. It is being developed by the Ratle Hydroelectric Power Corporation (RHPCL), a joint venture between the Jammu and Kashmir State Power Development Corporation (JKSPDC) and the National Hydroelectric Power Corporation (NHPC).
● The **Kishanganga Hydroelectric Project** is a 330 MW **run-of-the-river scheme in Jammu & Kashmir**,
which diverts water from the Kishanganga River to a power station in the **Jhelum River basin.** It is situated near Dharmahama village, about 5 km north of Bandipore in the Kashmir Valley. The project is expected to generate **1,713 million units of electricity annually**. Its dam diverts Jhelum waters to an underground powerhouse by transferring water from the **Gurez Valley** into mainland Kashmir, rather than allowing it to flow onward into Pakistan. **So, statements I and II are correct and statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India has rejected a ruling issued by the Court of Arbitration on the Kishenganga and Ratle hydroelectric projects situated in the Indian Union Territory of Jammu and Kashmir.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following countries:
I. - Uganda
II. - Zambia
III. Angola
IV. Cameroon
V. - Central African Republic
How many of the above share a land border with the Democratic Republic of Congo?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC)** lies in Central Africa, with the Equator running through it. Congo is bounded to the north by the **Central African Republic** and South Sudan; to the east by **Uganda,** Rwanda, Burundi and Tanzania; to the southeast by **Zambia**; and to the southwest by **Angola.** To the west are the country’s short Atlantic coastline, the Angolan exclave of Cabinda, and Congo (Brazzaville). **So, points I, II, III and V are correct.**
● Its major physiographic features include the **Congo River,** the country’s principal river system and the Katanga Plateau, characterised by extensive savanna grasslands interspersed with forested valleys. The highest point in the region is Mount Nabemba.
● **Cameroon** does not share a land border with the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). **So, point IV** **is not correct.**
**So, only four of the above share a land border with the Democratic Republic of Congo.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Democratic Republic of Congo and Rwanda signed a U.S.-brokered peace deal to end decades of conflict in eastern Congo.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText: The term " Global Minimum Tax”, recently seen in the news, refers to a:

options.a:
Uniform tax imposed by the United Nations
(UN) on all cross-border digital transactions to curb money laundering
options.b:
Minimum customs duty rate applied by
World Trade Organization (WTO) members on imported goods to ensure trade fairness
options.c:
Mandatory carbon tax set by Organisation for
Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) countries to discourage highemission industries
options.d:
Minimum corporate tax rate aimed at
preventing multinational companies from shifting profits to low-tax jurisdictions

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **“Global Minimum Tax” (GMT)** is an internationally agreed-upon **minimum rate of taxes** that would be paid by **large corporations**. The new GMT sets a proposed rate of 15% on profits. The **Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)** proposal has received the support of 137 countries and was approved at the October 2021 Summit in Rome with an effective date of 2024.
● It will ensure that Multinational Enterprises (MNEs) with revenues above EUR 750 million are subject to a 15% effective minimum tax rate wherever they operate. The GMT is designed to **reduce the incentive** **for businesses to shift profits to countries with lower tax rates** and to help ensure that all countries receive a fair share of the taxes paid by multinational companies.
● It is a historical agreement that aims to address the tax challenges arising from the globalisation and digitalisation of the economy. Since then, the implementation of the GMT has progressed with around 55 jurisdictions already taking steps toward implementation and with the rules coming into effect in 2024. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the G7 group has agreed to exempt U.S. multinationals from the global minimum tax.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: With reference to the rivers joining the Godavari river from West to East, which one of the following sequences is correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Godavari basin** extends over the States of Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh, Chhattisgarh and Odisha in addition to smaller parts in Madhya Pradesh, Karnataka and the Union Territory of Puducherry having a total area of 3,12,812 Sq.km with a maximum length and width of about 995 km and 583 km.
● The **Godavari River rises from Trimbakeshwar** in the Nashik district of Maharashtra About 338 km from the origin, the river receives the combined waters from the **Purna and Dudhna**.
● The **river Pranhita,** conveying the combined waters of Penganga, the Wardha and Wainganga, which drain Nagpur and the southern slopes of the Satpura ranges, falls into the **Godavari about 306 km**.
● The **Indravati River** originates from Kalahandi, Odisha. After flowing through Chhattisgarh, the Indravati River turns south, marking the boundary between Chhattisgarh and Maharashtra before **joining** **the Godavari River at the tri-state junction of Maharashtra, Chhattisgarh, and Telangana.**
● The largest tributary of the Godavari is the **Pranhita,** with about **34.87%** coverage of the drainage area.
● The **Pravara, Manjira and Maner** are right bank tributaries covering about 16.14%, the **Purna,** **Pranhita, Dudhna, Indravathi and Sabari** are important left bank tributaries, covering nearly 59.7% of the total catchment area of the basin.
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A new dispute has emerged between Telangana and Andhra Pradesh regarding the Polavaram– Banakacherla Link Project located on the Godavari River.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD):
I. - It is an informal strategic dialogue established by India, USA, Japan and South Korea.
II. - Unlike NATO, the Quad does not include provisions for collective defence.
III. The Malabar Exercise is conducted annually
with participation from all QUAD countries.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD) is the informal strategic dialogue between India, USA, Japan and Australia. It aimed at strengthening **regional security and economic cooperation** in the **Indo-** **Pacific.**  **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The QUAD is not structured like a typical multilateral organisation, and lacks a secretariat and any permanent decision-making body (like the EU or UN). Additionally, unlike NATO, the **QUAD does not** **include provisions for collective defence,** instead choosing to conduct joint military exercises as a show of unity and diplomatic cohesion. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Malabar series of exercises** began as an **annual bilateral naval exercise** between **India and the** **US in 1992**. **Japan** joined the Naval Exercises in **2015.** **Malabar 2020** saw the participation of the
**Australian Navy also**. In 2020, the trilateral India-US-Japan Malabar naval exercises expanded to include Australia, marking the first official grouping of the QUAD since its resurgence in 2017 and the first joint military exercises among the four countries in over a decade. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The coast guards of the **QUAD nations**—India, Japan, the United States and Australiahave launched the **QUAD at Sea Ship Observer Mission** to strengthen maritime cooperation in the Indo-Pacific. This observer-at-sea initiative is the first of its kind involving the Indian Coast Guard, Japan Coast Guard, U.S. Coast Guard and the Australian Border Force.
**Relevance:** Recently, the QUAD nations launched a coast guard cooperation initiative.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
On which of the following grounds can the Election Commission of India (ECI) de-list a political party in India?
I. - If the party does not contest elections continuously for six years
II. - If the party fails to submit audited accounts for three consecutive years
III. If a party fields majority candidates with
criminal track records
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The right to form an association is a **Fundamental Right** guaranteed under **Article 19(1)(c) of the** **Constitution of India to all citizens.** Political parties are an association or body of individuals that can be formed by citizens.
● **Representation of the People Act (RPA), 1951** lays down the requirements for **registration of a political** **party with the Election Commission of India (ECI).**
○ Any political party that seeks registration should submit a copy of its memorandum/constitution within 30 days of its formation. Such a document should contain a provision that the party shall bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India. It should also bear allegiance to the principles of socialism, secularism and democracy, and uphold the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India.
● Guidelines for registration of Political Parties mention that if the party does not contest elections continuously for 6 years, the party shall be taken off the list of registered parties. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The ECI does not have the power to de-register a political party solely for fielding a majority of candidates with criminal track records. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Non-submission of audited accounts** by the political party does not give ECI power to de-list; it only leads to withdrawal of benefits like tax exemption. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The RPA **does not confer explicit powers on the ECI to de-register any political** **party** if it fails to contest elections, conduct inner-party elections or lodge requisite returns.
● The Supreme Court in **Indian National Congress versus Institute of Social Welfare** **& Ors (2002)** had held that the ECI does not have the power to de-register any political party under the RPA. It may be deregistered only under exceptional circumstances, such as the registration being **obtained by fraud or the political** **party ceasing to have allegiance to the Indian Constitution or if it is declared** **unlawful by the Government.**
**Relevance:** The Election Commission of India (ECI) has initiated steps to de-list 345 Registered Unrecognised Political Parties (RUPPs) that have not contested elections in the last six years.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Thiruchendur temple in Tamil Nadu:
I. - It is built in the traditional Dravidian style of architecture.
II. - It is the only temple in India with a Raja Gopuram on the western side.
III. It is among the six Abodes of Lord Murugan
mentioned in the Sangam literature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Thiruchendur Murugan Temple**, also known as **Sri Subramaniya Swamy Temple**, is one of the most revered temples dedicated to **Lord Murugan** (also known as Kartikeya or Subramaniya) in Tamil Nadu, India.
● The **six most sacred abodes of Murugan was mentioned in Tamil sangam literature**, "Thirumurugatrupadai", written by Nakkeerar and in "Thiruppugazh", written by Arunagirinathar. The six abodes are Thiruparankundram, **Tiruchendur**, Palani, Swamimalai, Thiruthani and Pazhamudircholai. These six abodes are called Aarupadai Veedu which in translation means the "six war houses of the Lord". **So, statement III is correct.**
● Over the centuries, the temple received support from major **Tamil dynasties** such as the **Pandyas,** **Cheras, Cholas and Vijayanagar rulers**, each adding to its growth and splendour. Unlike many others, **it was not built by kings but by three revered saints.**
● The temple features classical **Dravidian architectural elements,** including **intricate carvings, spacious** **pillared halls and imposing gopurams (gateway towers).** **So, statement I is correct.**
● Another unique feature of this temple is that, unlike all the other temples in India which have its Raja Gopuram at the eastern side, **Tiruchendur temple alone has its Raja Gopuram at the western side.** The main sanctum enshrines Lord Murugan in a dynamic warrior stance, flanked by his consorts, Valli and Deivanai. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Supreme Court refused to hear Vidhayahar’s plea claiming Tamil Nadu officials unilaterally set the Tiruchendur Murugan Temple’s Kumbhabhishekam.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: Candida tropicalis, sometimes seen in the news, is a:

options.a:
Bacterial pathogen commonly implicated in
hospital-acquired pneumonia
options.b:
Fungal pathogen associated with opportunistic
infections
options.c:
Protozoan parasite responsible for diarrhoeal
diseases in tropical regions
options.d:
Viral strain known to cause recurrent outbreaks
of viral fever in coastal regions

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Candida tropicalis** is an important **fungal pathogen in India** and many parts of the world. Its infections are associated with a **mortality rate of 55-60%.** Anti-fungal drugs called azoles, such as fluconazole and voriconazole, are used to treat its infections. But there is a growing concern in medical circles that clinics are seeing an increasing number of infections by strains of C. tropicalis that exhibit high resistance to these drugs.
● Candida tropicalis is an **opportunistic fungus that affects immunocompromised individuals** and can spread to vital organs. It has emerged as one of the most important Candida species. It has been widely considered the second-most virulent Candida species, only surpassed by C. albicans. Besides, this species has been recognised as a very strong biofilm producer, surpassing C. albicans in most of the studies. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** ‘Candida tropicalis’ has been found to use chromosome alterations to resist common anti-fungal drugs such as fluconazole and voriconazole.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following chemicals:
I. - Bisphenol A
II. - Dioxins
III. Polyethylene glycol
IV. Triclosan
V. - Vinegar
How many of the above are Endocrine-Disrupting Chemicals (EDCs)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Endocrine-Disrupting Chemicals (EDCs)** are natural or human-made chemicals that may mimic, block, or interfere with the body’s hormones, which are part of the endocrine system. These chemicals are associated with a wide array of health issues.
● Endocrine disruptors are found in many everyday products, including some cosmetics, food and beverage packaging, toys, carpet, and pesticides. Some chemicals that act as flame retardants may also be endocrine disruptors.
● According to the Endocrine Society, there are nearly 85,000 human-made chemicals in the world, and 1,000 or more of those could be endocrine disruptors, based on their unique properties. The following are among the most common and well-studied.
○ **Bisphenol A (BPA)** is used to make polycarbonate plastics and epoxy resins. It is used in manufacturing, food packaging, toys, and other applications. BPA resins may be found in the lining of some canned foods and beverages. **So, point I is correct.** ○ **Dioxins** are a byproducts of certain manufacturing processes, such as herbicide production and paper bleaching.
