They can be released into the air from waste burning and wildfires. **So, point II** **is correct.** ○ **Triclosan** is an ingredient that was previously added to some antimicrobial and personal care products, like liquid body wash and soaps. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Polyethylene glycol (PEG)** is a synthetic polymer used in cosmetics, medications, and food processing. Its main concerns relate to possible impurities (e.g., ethylene oxide, 1,4-dioxane), not hormone disruption. **PEG itself does not interfere with hormone systems**. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Vinegar** is simply a dilute solution of acetic acid, a natural compound produced by fermentation. It has **no known hormone-disrupting** **effects** in humans or animals. **So, point V is not correct.** **So, only three of the above are Endocrine-Disrupting Chemicals (EDCs).** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge box**
**Other Endocrine-Disrupting Chemicals (EDCs) include:**
● **Perchlorate** is a colorless salt manufactured and used as an industrial chemical to make rockets, explosives, and fireworks, which can be found in some groundwater.
● **Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS)** are a large group of chemicals used widely in industrial applications, such as firefighting foam, nonstick pans, paper, and textile coatings.
● **Phthalates** are a large group of compounds used as liquid plasticizers. They are found in hundreds of products including some food packaging, cosmetics, fragrances, children’s toys, and medical device tubing.
● **Phytoestrogens** are naturally occurring substances with hormone-like activity found in some plants; they may have a similar effect to estrogen produced by the body. Soy foods, for example, contain phytoestrogens.
● **Polybrominated diphenyl ethers** (PBDE) are used to make flame retardants for products such as furniture foam and carpet.
● **Polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs)** were used to make electrical equipment, such as transformers, and are in hydraulic fluids, heat transfer fluids, lubricants, and plasticizers. PCBs were mass-produced globally until they were banned in 1979.
● **Atrazine** is one of the most commonly applied herbicides in the world, often used to control weeds in corn, sorghum, and sugarcane crops.
**Relevance:** Plastic pollution is no longer a distant environmental concern; it is a biological invasion with profound implications for human health.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText: Missing Q42
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● T cells, a critical component of the immune system, mature in the thymus. The thymus acts as a quality control centre, allowing T cells that recognise foreign pathogens to survive while **eliminating those that** **would attack the body’s own tissues**. This process of weeding out self-reactive T cells is termed ‘**central** **tolerance**’.
● Even though central tolerance removes most self-reactive lymphocytes, a few escape into the circulation. **Peripheral immune tolerance** is the set of mechanisms that keep mature immune cells from attacking the body after they have already left the thymus and bone marrow.
○ It serves as the **second layer of protection against autoimmunity**. Peripheral tolerance prevents these cells from causing harm by inactivating them (anergy), making them undergo apoptosis, or suppressing them through regulatory T cells (Tregs).
● Tregs help control inflammation, prevent excessive tissue damage during infections, support wound healing, and influence metabolism. Consequently, Treg dysregulation is implicated in a range of diseases, from autoimmune conditions like type 1 diabetes and multiple sclerosis to cancers and chronic infections where the immune response is insufficient. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**T cells – essential players in the body’s defence:**
● Helper T cells constantly patrol the body. If they discover an invading microbe, they alert other immune cells, which then mount an attack.
● Killer T cells eradicate cells which have been infected by a virus or other pathogens. They can also attack tumour cells.
● All T cells have special proteins called T-cell receptors on their surface. These receptors can be likened to a type of sensor. Using them, T cells can scan other cells to discover whether the body is under attack.
**Relevance:** The 2025 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to Mary E. Brunkow, Fred Ramsdell, and Shimon Sakaguchi for their discoveries concerning peripheral immune tolerance.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText: Missing Q43
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Quantum mechanics** allows a particle to move straight through a barrier, using a process called tunnelling. As soon as large numbers of particles are involved, quantum mechanical effects usually become insignificant. The 2025 Physics Nobel laureates’ experiments demonstrated that quantum mechanical properties can be made concrete on a macroscopic scale.
● **Quantum tunnelling** is a fundamental quantum mechanical effect where particles behave like waves. Due to this wave-like nature, there is a finite probability of **finding a particle on the other side of a** **potential barrier**, **even when its energy should not allow it to cross**. This principle underpins technologies such as **tunnel diodes, quantum computing elements, and scanning tunneling** **microscopes**.
● In superconducting quantum circuits, electrons pair into Cooper pairs and behave collectively as a single quantum object. Their energy levels become quantised, forming potential wells. Due to macroscopic quantum tunneling, these quantised states can “leak” through barriers between wells, enabling superposition and state transitions essential for qubit operations. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** John Clarke, Michel H. Devoret, and John M. Martinis have been awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics, 2025 for the discovery of macroscopic quantum mechanical tunnelling and energy quantisation in an electric circuit.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText: Missing Q44
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs)** are like crystalline scaffolds built from two ingredients: metals that act like connective joints and organic – that is, carbon-based molecules that behave as bridges to link those joints in a repeating pattern. The result is a highly ordered, porous framework, a kind of molecular architecture that’s both sturdy and full of empty space.
● These materials have unprecedented chemical and structural tunability, exceptional thermal stability, ultrahigh porosity, and a large surface area, making them an ideal candidate for numerous potential applications.
● **Applications of MOFs:** ○ **Carbon capture and gas separation:** MOFs can adsorb and trap greenhouse gases like CO₂, e.g. from flue gases of power plants, helping reduce emissions. **So, point I is correct.** ○ **Water harvesting or wastewater treatment:** Some MOFs can adsorb water vapour from air (even at low humidity), enabling atmospheric water harvesting (AWH), potentially a way to obtain fresh water in water-scarce regions. **So, point II is correct.** ○ **Gas storage:** Because of their high porosity and large internal surface area, MOFs can store gases, such as hydrogen or methane, potentially useful for **clean-energy storage** or fuel systems. **So,** **point III is correct.**
○ **Biomedical applications (drug delivery, sensors, etc.):** The porous cavities of MOFs can encapsulate drug molecules, enabling controlled drug-release systems. MOFs have also been explored for biosensing, imaging, and other biomedical uses. **So, point IV is correct.** ○ **Catalysis:** MOFs can act as catalysts or catalyst supports (because of their functional metal centres and high surface area), facilitating chemical reactions including conversions useful for industry or environmental cleanup. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Richard Robson, Susumu Kitagawa, and Omar Yaghi — have been honoured with the Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 2025.

questions.subject: Polity

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Cooperatives** are people-centered enterprises owned, controlled and run by and for their members to realise their common economic, social and cultural needs and aspirations.
● Every year, the **International Day of Cooperatives** is observed on the first Saturday of July. The United Nations declared **2025 as the International Year of Cooperatives**. This year, the world celebrated International Day of Cooperatives on 5th July. The theme for International Cooperatives Day 2025 was “Cooperatives Build a Better World.” **So, statement I is correct.**
○ CoopsDay, or International Day of Cooperatives was first celebrated in 1923 and officially recognized by the UN in 1995. It highlights how cooperatives help people work together, build stronger and self-reliant communities, and promote the values of democracy, solidarity, and sustainability.
● The Union Minister inaugurated the country's first national-level cooperative university, “Tribhuvan Sahkari University (TSU)” at Anand in Gujarat in 2025. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **In July 2025, the Government unveiled the National Cooperation Policy 2025** to meet the nation’s goal of becoming “Viksit” by 2047. Rooted in the ethos of Sahkar-se-Samriddhi, this policy aims to build on the unique strengths of India’s cooperative tradition, promote economic democratization, and uplift rural economies through collective participation. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The Indian Farmers Fertiliser Cooperative Limited (IFFCO) and **Gujarat Cooperative Milk Marketing** **Federation (Amul), have been ranked as the top cooperatives in the world** based on GDP per capita performance in 2025. The ranking has been done by the International Cooperative Alliance (ICA) World Cooperative Monitor 2025. The list was announced at the ICA CM50 Conference in Doha, Qatar.  **So,** **statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Union Cooperation Minister laid the foundation stone for India’s first national-level cooperative university, ‘Tribhuvan Sahkari University’ (TSU), in Anand, Gujarat.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText: Missing Q46
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **International Treaty on Plant Genetic Resources for Food and Agriculture (ITPGRFA)** is a **legally binding** agreement adopted under the FAO in **2001.** Its purpose is to conserve, sustainably use, and ensure fair sharing of benefits arising from plant genetic resources crucial for food and agriculture. It establishes a Multilateral System (MLS) that facilitates global access to plant germplasm for research, breeding, and food security. S**o, statement II is correct.**
● The Treaty explicitly aims to conserve plant genetic resources, promote their sustainable use, and facilitate benefit-sharing. It was adopted by **FAO** and not by the International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI). **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **India is a contracting party** and actively engages in its decision-making, including the Multilateral System (MLS) negotiations. MLS **provides access to a list of important crops for research and** **breeding**, while ensuring that benefits from their use are shared among the signatories. **So, statements** **III and IV are correct.**
● There is a proposal to amend **Annex 1** of the International Treaty on Plant Genetic Resources for Food and Agriculture (ITPGRFA) to bring **all Plant Genetic Resources for Food and Agriculture (PGRFA)** under the Multilateral System (MLS) of access and benefit-sharing.
○ Currently, only a selected list of crops is covered under Annex 1. The proposed change seeks to expand this to **include all crops and plant genetic resources** that have actual or potential value for food and agriculture without exceptions. ○ This would force member countries, including India, to place their entire national germplasm collections into the global MLS and share them under the standard Material Transfer Agreement (SMTA) determined by the Treaty’s Governing Body. ○ Indian scientists, farmer groups, and State governments argue that such expansion would **undermine India’s sovereign rights** over its plant genetic resources. It would also dilute farmers’ traditional seed rights**.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Scientists, policymakers and activists from India have flagged concerns over the proposal to amend the International Treaty on Plant Genetic Resources for Food and Agriculture.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText: Missing Q47
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Gini Index** is one of the most widely used global statistical measures to assess **income or wealth** **inequality** within a country. It ranges from **0 to 100**, where **0 signifies perfect equality**, and **100 indicates** **absolute inequality**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● As per the 2025 World Bank report, India’s Gini Index stands at **25.5**, making it the **fourth most equal** **country in the world** after the Slovak Republic, Slovenia and Belarus. **So, Statement III is not correct.**
● In comparison, the **United States** has a Gini Index of **41.8** (much higher inequality). Thus, India is **significantly better placed** in terms of equality compared to major economies like the U.S., U.K., and China. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India’s Gini Index score stands at 25.5, with onlythe  Slovak Republic, Slovenia and Belarus faring better.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText: Missing Q48
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Vera C. Rubin Observatory is one of the world’s most advanced wide-field astronomical survey facilities. Built to conduct the **Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST)**, it aims to capture unprecedented wide-angle, ultra-high-resolution images of the universe.
● The VRO has been constructed on one of the peaks of **Cerro Pachón**, a mountain in northern **Chile.** This region is known for exceptionally clear skies, and minimal light pollution, ideal conditions for astronomical observations.
● **The telescope has the largest digital camera in the world.** It is the size of a small car, weighs 2,800 kg, and boasts a staggering resolution of 3,200 megapixels. The camera can produce an image so rich in detail that it would take a wall of 400 ultrahigh-definition TV screens to display it in full.
● The VRO is designed for rapid, repeated **scanning of the entire southern sky**. Its advanced imaging system compares each new image with a baseline “general image” of that region.
● If a change such as a moving asteroid, a brightening star, or a new supernova is detected, the system can issue an alert within **60 seconds**. Once fully operational, the VRO is expected to produce **around 10** **million alerts every night**, representing the world’s most powerful real-time sky-monitoring capability. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The first images from the Vera Rubin Observatory show a detailed view of 10 million galaxies, thousands of new asteroids, and stars changing brightness

questions.subject: Economy

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Zero-G indicator – Joy”** refers to a **toy baby swan carried aboard the Axiom-4 mission’s Dragon** **spacecraft.** It is used to signal the exact moment the crew enters **zero gravity**. As soon as the spacecraft reaches orbit and weightlessness begins, the toy starts floating. It is an old tradition that **began with Yuri** **Gagarin,** the first human in space. Joy was selected as the Zero-G indicator because of astronaut **Shubhanshu Shukla’s son’s fondness for animals.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A new 'crew' member of the Axiom-4 mission, a toy swan named Joy,  was seen floating in the Dragon spacecraft.

questions.subject: Science & Technology

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Prithvi-II is a **short-range, surface-to-surface ballistic missile** developed under India’s Integrated Guided Missile Development Programme. The missile is operated by the Indian Armed Forces and plays a key role in India’s strategic deterrence capability. While Agni-I is a medium-range ballistic missile developed by India as part of the Agni series under the Integrated Guided Missile Development Programme. It is operated by the Strategic Forces Command.
● Prithvi-II has a range of about 350 km. Agni-I has a range of about 700–1,200 km, which is much longer. **So, option (a) is not correct.**
● **Agni-I has a range of 700–900 kilometres**. It has a payload capacity of 1,000 kilograms. **Prithvi-II have** **a range of 250–350 km** and a payload of up to 500 kg. Therefore, Agni-I is not lower than Prithvi-II. **So,** **option (b) is not correct.**
● **Prithvi-II is a single-stage, liquid-fuelled** short-range ballistic missile. **Agni-I is a single-stage, solid-** **fuelled** short-range ballistic missile. **So, option (c) is correct.**
● **Both Prithvi-II and Agni-I are nuclear-capable missiles.** They have been tested and inducted under India’s Strategic Forces Command for nuclear deterrence. **So, option (d) is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge box**
**About Strategic Forces Command:**
● The Strategic Forces Command (SFC) is **India's tri-service command** responsible for managing and administering the country's nuclear weapons stockpile. It was established in 2003 and is a key part of the **Nuclear Command Authority** (NCA),
which is responsible for all command, control, and decision-making for India's nuclear weapons program.
**Relevance:** Recently, the Strategic Forces Command successfully test-fired the short-range ballistic missiles Prithvi-II and Agni-I from the Integrated Test Range in Chandipur, Odisha.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText: Missing Q51
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A bow echo is essentially a line of storms, on the radar that looks like a bow. The term "bow echo" refers to **bands of rain showers or thunderstorms that "bow out**" when the storm's strong winds reach the surface and spread horizontally. Bow out means that it **assumes a curved or bowed shape. It looks like** **an archer's bow when seen on weather radar. So, option c is correct.**
● A bow echo can extend from 20 km to 100 km, and last between three and six hours.
● The term ‘Bow Echo’ was coined in the 1970s by Ted Fujita. If the bow echo progresses more than 400 kilometers with wind speed of 93 km/h or greater, then the bow echo is classified as a derecho.
● **Formation of bow echo:** ○ When rain-cooled air comes down to the ground, and spreads out horizontally, a boundary called the gust front is created between the rain-cooled air and warm-moist air on the surface. ○ This front pushes up the warm-moist air into the atmosphere, which forms new thunderstorms. These new thunderstorms produce more rain, thereby creating more rain-cooled air, which helps the gust front to maintain its strength. ○ As this process keeps repeating itself, there comes a point when there is an inflow of air on the trailing side of the line of storms and bends it like an archer’s bow. At this point, there is a pronounced bow in the storm, as seen by Doppler radar. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: An intense storm that recently hit Delhi, appeared in the form of “bow echoes”.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText: Missing Q52
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● In the Indian context, soon after the independence, Government of India set up a Committee on Fair Wages in 1948 which has defined various concepts of wages which govern the wage structure in the country specially in those sectors which can be termed as underpaid and where workers do not have bargaining power through unions. These concepts are: minimum wage, living wage, and fair wage. Later, the concept of need-based minimum wage was added.
● A **living wage** is the pay a worker earns in a normal work week that is **enough to provide a decent** **standard of living** for them and their family. It covers basic needs like food, housing, healthcare, education, and transport, and ensures a dignified life beyond just survival. There is **no legal requirement** for employers to pay a living wage. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● The **minimum wage** is the **legally fixed lowest pay** fixed by the government that employers must give workers for their work in a set time. It serves as a legal safeguard against exploitation. However, it **does** **not always consider** the **cost of living, family needs**, or a decent standard of living. **So, pair II is** **correctly matched.**
● **Fair wage is the wage which is above the minimum wage but below the living wage**. The lower limit of the fair wage is obviously the minimum wage: the upper limit is to be set by the capacity of the industry to pay. Thus, fair wage depends on different variables affecting wage determination. Such factors are labour productivity prevailing wage rates, the level of national income and its distribution and the capacity of industry to pay. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The government is currently considering a ‘living wage’, and its impact could be especially significant for women at work, as they often face higher opportunity costs.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Rinderpest, also known as cattle plague, **is a contagious viral disease affecting clovenhoofed animals** (mainly cattle and buffalo). Rinderpest is spread by effective contact between an animal carrying the virus and a susceptible animal. The virus is found in nasal secretions and other bodily fluids. The **Rinderpest** **does not affect Humans. So, statement I is correct.**
● **Rinderpest** was one of the most destructive livestock diseases in history **before its global eradication in** **2011.** In 2011, Rinderpest became the second disease to be declared officially eradicated, after smallpox in 1980.
○ **India was declared rinderpest-free in May 2006 by the World Organisation for Animal** **Health (OIE). So, statement II is correct.**
● Rinderpest Virus-Containing Material (RVCM) remains only in a few laboratories across the globe. To preserve global freedom from the disease, FAO and WOAH have implemented strict measures to limit the storage of RVCM to a small number of high-security laboratories worldwide. India has designated ICAR-National Institute of High Security Animal Diseases (NIHSAD) in Bhopal, as its national repository for RVCM. **So, statement III is correct.**
○ ICAR-National Institute of High Security Animal Diseases (NIHSAD) Bhopal  has been recently recognized as a Category A Rinderpest Holding Facility (RHF) by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH) and the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) of the United Nations. ○ This recognition places India among a distinguished group of only six facilities worldwide entrusted with the critical responsibility of securely holding rinderpest virus material. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** ICAR-National Institute of High Security Animal Diseases Bhopal has been recently recognized as a Category A Rinderpest Holding Facility by the World Organisation for Animal Health and the Food and Agriculture Organization.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements in respect of
the ICC Women's ODI World Cup:
I. 	 It was first played in 1973, two years before
the inaugural men’s World Cup,
II. 	 England is the most successful team in the
history of the Women's World Cup.
III. 	The 2025 edition was the first time India
hosted the event.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The ICC Women’s ODI World Cup was first played in 1973, two years before the inaugural men’s World Cup. It is the oldest global event in the sport. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Australia is the most successful team in the history of the ICC Women's Cricket World Cup, with seven titles, while England is the second-most successful with four wins. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The 2025 World Cup was the 13th edition of the ICC Women's Cricket World Cup. The final was held at Navi Mumbai’s D.Y. Patil Stadium. The 13 editions have been hosted in six different countries with **India** **holding the showpiece four times --** including one as co-host. Sri Lanka (co-hosts), England, New Zealand, Australia and South Africa have been the other hosts. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **India defeated South Africa** by 52 runs in the Finals of the ICC Women's ODI World Cup 2025. **India** **thus won its first-ever ICC Women’s ODI World Cup.** This was the third time India had reached the final of the Women's World Cup, having finished runners-up in 2005 and 2017. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, India defeated South Africa by 52 runs in the Finals of the ICC Women's ODI World Cup 2025.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Space Docking:
I. 	 The Axiom-4 mission of the United States
was the first to successfully demonstrate spacecraft docking mechanism.
II. 	 India is the fourth country after the U.S.,
Russia and China to conduct the docking experiment.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Docking is a process by which two fast-moving spacecraft are brought to the same orbit, brought closer to each other manually or autonomously, and finally joined together. This capability is necessary for carrying out missions that require heavy spacecraft that a single launch vehicle may not be capable of lifting off with.
● On March 16, 1966 Gemini VIII, commanded by Neil Armstrong with pilot David Scott, achieved the **world’s first docking in orbit** with the Agena Target Vehicle. It was the first successful spacecraft-tospacecraft docking. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Axiom-4 SpaceX Dragon** spacecraft, carrying Indian astronaut Shubhanshu Shukla and three others, conducted a rendezvous with the International Space Station and **completed a successful docking.**
● **India has recently joined the United States, Russia (including the erstwhile Soviet Union), and China** **in demonstrating in-orbit docking capability**, making it the fourth nation with proven space docking competence. The docking was part of the SpaDeX mission, a significant milestone for India’s space exploration program. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Indian astronaut Shubhanshu Shukla’s Dragon spacecraft docked with the ISS.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With reference to Amateur (HAM) Radio, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 It uses two way radio stations to communicate
with others similar to voice calls.
II. 	 It can be used to provide reliable communications during disasters.
III. ​	Any individual above the age of 12 can use a
HAM in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Amateur radio,** popularly known as **HAM radio,** is a licensed radio service that relies on radio waves for establishing communication. Ham radio service is largely deployed for educational and knowledge purposes, for emergency or SOS communication. Using a dedicated frequency, a transceiver and an antenna, communication can be set up between two licensed hams by trained ham operators.
● **Amateur radio operators use two way radio stations** and communicate with others **similarly** **authorized using various modes of communication like voice**, morse code, computers, internet etc. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The core application of amateur radio is **emergency communication** and disaster relief, where trained operators provide backup links when conventional networks are disrupted. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The **Wireless Planning & Coordination (WPC) Wing** of the Ministry of Communications, Department of Telecommunications, is the regulatory authority for amateur radio in India. In India, any individual above the **age of 12 is permitted to operate an Amateur radio.** WPC is responsible for issuing licenses, allocating frequencies, and monitoring compliance with technical and operational norms. **So, statement** **III is correct.**
● It was first used on a space shuttle to establish communication between space and Earth in 1983. The ISS also has a ham radio, known as the Amateur Radio on the International Space Station (ARISS), which is often used to organise astronaut-student interactions. The objective of such interactions is to inspire the young generation.
● Amateur radio communication can range from **hyper-local communication (short distances) to global** **communication through the ionosphere and even communication involving space via amateur** **satellites and the International Space Station.** This makes amateur radio a versatile communication mode ranging from simple local contacts to long-distance and space-based communication. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Indian astronaut Shubhanshu Shukla will today (July 4) interact with the country’s students using a ham radio from the International Space Station (ISS).

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Securities Transaction Tax (STT):
I. 	 It is an indirect tax levied on securities
transactions through a listed stock exchange.
II. 	 It is the only tax in India imposed solely on
the act of carrying out a profession.
III. 	It functions similarly to TDS for salaried
individuals with a refund option at year-end.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Securities Transaction Tax (STT) is a **direct tax** levied and collected by the central government **on** **securities transactions through a listed stock exchange.** It was introduced in the Finance Act 2004 as an improved method to simplify capital gains reporting and tax collection, aiming to reduce tax evasion. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● STT is levied on profits from financial instruments, such as equities, futures, and options, or the purchase/sale of any securities listed on a stock market exchange. It is the **only tax in India levied solely** **on the act of conducting a profession**, and it had to be paid regardless of profit, making it almost punitive or deterrent in nature. **So, statement II is correct.**
● For stock market participants**, STT functions similarly to TDS for salaried individuals.** However, while TDS is refunded at year-end or credited against income tax, there is no such provision for STT, and traders must pay it **without any refund options. So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The Supreme Court of India decided to examine a plea challenging the constitutional validity of the Securities Transaction Tax.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are examples of primary
aerosols?
I. 	 Sea salt particles
II. 	 Sulphate aerosols
III. 	Mineral dust
IV. 	 Nitrate aerosols
V. 	 Volcanic ash
Select the correct answer using the code given below.

options.b: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Aerosols** are tiny particles suspended in the atmosphere. They range in size from a few tens of nanometres—smaller than the width of the smallest viruses—to several tens of micrometres—about the diameter of a human hair—and have a significant impact on climate, weather, health, and ecology.
● Aerosols injected directly into the atmosphere are referred to as **'primary aerosols'. These include sea** **salt particles, mineral dust, smoke, and volcanic ash**. **So, points I, III, and V are correct.**
● **Secondary aerosols** are formed from gases emitted in other forms that undergo chemical reactions in the atmosphere, such as **sulfate aerosols from volcanoes** or industrial emissions, and **nitrate aerosols** from fossil fuel use and agricultural fertilisation. All aerosols can also experience further chemical changes, known as ‘ageing’ effects. **So, point II and IV are not correct.**
● Sulfate and nitrate aerosols result primarily from the **oxidation of sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides**. They form the major component of particulate matter (**PM2.5**) and cause respiratory issues (asthma, reduced lung function), cardiovascular problems (heart attacks, stroke), inflammation, and increasing mortality, especially for vulnerable groups. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A recent study at IIT Bhubaneswar has found a shielding phenomenon in some cities in the northwestern and northern Indo-Gangetic Plain against long-range aerosol pollution.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
The Kaladan Multimodal Transit Transport project
will connect:

options.a:
India’s western coast to Iran via the Arabian
Sea
options.b:
India’s peninsular ports to Sri Lanka via the
Palk Strait
options.c:
India’s integrated check posts to Nepal and
Bhutan
options.d:
India’s eastern ports to Myanmar via the
North-East

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**:
● **The Kaladan Multimodal Transit Transport Project** aims to provide an alternative **connection to** **North East India from the Kolkata/Haldia port through Sittwe Port and the Kaladan River up to** **Paletwa in Myanmar by waterway, and from Paletwa to Zorinpui via road in Mizoram.** It also offers an alternative route between the landlocked North East and the rest of India.
● Sittwe Port is a deepwater port situated in Sittwe, the capital of **Myanmar's Rakhine State**. This port, developed and funded by India, is strategically located at the estuary of the Kaladan River, on the Bay of Bengal. Utilising this port through the Kaladan project forms a major vector of India’s **‘Act East Policy**’.
● The Ministry of External Affairs is the main authority responsible for implementation. The Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI) is the Project Development Consultant (PDC) for this initiative. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the Union Minister for Ports, Shipping, and Waterways announced that the Kaladan project is expected to be operational by 2027.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Azerbaijan has recently normalised relations with Armenia after decades of conflict.
**Statement II:** Russia has facilitated diplomatic normalisation as part of its ongoing regional policy in the South Caucasus.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In August 2025, **Armenia and Azerbaijan signed a joint declaration in Washington, committing to** **normalise relations**, which includes establishing diplomatic ties, opening transport routes, and forming a bilateral commission for the implementation of the peace agreement. The process was years in the making and is part of a comprehensive peace agreement after decades of conflict. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Although Russia had previously played a significant mediating role, especially in the agreements after the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh conflict, the most **recent peace deal and diplomatic normalisation were** **facilitated by the United States**, not Russia. The pivotal summit took place in Washington, D.C., with the participation of the US President. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In August 2025, Azerbaijan and Armenia signed a U.S.-brokered peace agreement during a meeting with U.S. President Donald Trump.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about Nidhi
Companies:
I. 	 A Nidhi company acts as a Non-Banking
Financial Company (NBFC) in India.
II. 	 A minimum of ₹10 lakh in net-owned funds
is required to register as a Nidhi Company.
III. 	A Nidhi Company must have at least 200
shareholders within one year of registration.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● A Nidhi company is a type of non-banking financial company (NBFC) in India that focuses on borrowing and lending money among its members, who are also shareholders. Nidhi Companies are governed by the **Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA)** under the **Companies Act, 2013**. They are classified as “Mutual Benefit Societies”. Although they deal with deposits, they are **exempt from RBI registration**. Therefore, they **do not require an RBI licence**. **So, statement I is correct**.
● To register as a Nidhi Company, a **minimum of ₹10 lakh in net-owned funds is required.** This includes equity share capital and free reserves minus any accumulated losses and intangible assets. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● A Nidhi Company **must have at least 200 shareholders** within one year of registration. This is mandatory for maintaining the company’s registration status. **So, statement III is correct.**
● As per the Nidhi Rules, 2014, Nidhi companies are **prohibited from opening current accounts for** **members**. They only offer services such as savings accounts, fixed deposits, and recurring deposits, which are available **only to their members**.
● The core principle of a Nidhi Company is “members only”. Under Nidhi Rules, they **can accept deposits** **and provide loans only to their members**, not to the public or businesses. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the Registrar of Companies has imposed penalties on over two dozen Nidhi companies for breaches of the Companies Act.

questions.subject: Current Affairs

questions.questionText:
The terms 'Balco and Tulda', sometimes mentioned
in the news recently, are related to:

options.a:
Bamboo species used for bio-ethanol
production
options.b: Varieties of freshwater fish in Indian rivers
options.c: Traditional tribal festivals of Northeast India
options.d:
Indigenous medicinal plants of the Western
Ghats

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Balco refers to **Bambusa balcooa**, a commercially important bamboo species found mainly in eastern and northeastern India, which is widely used for construction, handicrafts, pulp, and other purposes due to its strong, thick-walled culms.
● Tulda refers to **Bambusa tulda** (Indian timber/Bengal bamboo), a major bamboo species of the Northeastern part of India, used in construction, furniture, mats, and handicrafts due to its strength and versatility.
● These varieties were highlighted in the context of the **world’s largest bamboo-based ethanol plant,** inaugurated in Golaghat, Assam. The facility sources bamboo feedstock primarily from the Northeast, with a preference for the Balco (Bambusa balcooa) and Tulda (Bambusa tulda) varieties due to their high cellulose content, which makes them efficient for ethanol production. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, PM Modi inaugurated India’s first bamboo-based ethanol plant in Assam.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, Biosphere Reserve
questions.questionText:
The Dugong Conservation Reserve, India’s first of
its kind, is located in:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Dugong Conservation Reserve** was notified by the Tamil Nadu government under the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972, in 2022. It is India’s first dedicated reserve for dugongs. It is located in the **northern** **Palk Bay**, across parts of the Thanjavur and Pudukkottai districts, an area known for its extensive seagrass meadows that cover over 12,250 hectares. These seagrass beds form the critical habitat and feeding grounds for the dugong. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge box**
**Key facts about Dugong:**
● Dugongs (Dugong dugon) are the **only herbivorous mammals found in India’s marine** **ecosystems.** It is known as the **sea cow**, but resembles a cross between a seal and a whale, and is distributed through the Indo-Pacific region.
● They are **found along the Indian coastline**, primarily inhabiting warm waters around the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, the Gulf of Mannar, Palk Bay, and the Gulf of Kutch. The dugong is a long-lived species, able to live up to 70 years.
● Dugongs are **restricted to shallow waters**, where they spend the day feeding on seagrasses of the genera Cymodocea, Halophila, Thalassia, and Halodule.
● Individuals reach **reproductive maturity after only nine or ten years** and can give birth at intervals of around three to five years. Due to its slow reproductive cycle, extended time to
maturity, and infrequent calving, a dugong population’s maximum potential growth rate is estimated to be just about 5% per year.
● **Conservation status of Dugong:** ○ **IUCN:** Vulnerable ○ **CITES:** Appendix I ○ **Wildlife Protection Act 1972:** Schedule I
**Relevance:** Recently, the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) formally adopted a motion recognising India’s first Dugong Conservation Reserve in Palk Bay.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With reference to large-scale genome sequencing
initiatives, consider the following statements:
I. - The Genome India Project has created the first pan-India reference genome catalogue that shows the country’s genetic diversity.
II. 	 Gujarat has become the first Indian State to
launch a genome sequencing initiative focused exclusively on tribal communities.
III. 	 The Human Genome Project successfully
mapped and sequenced the entire human genome for the first time.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Genome India Project, funded by the Department of Biotechnology under the Ministry of Science and Technology, was a pioneering national initiative aimed at driving a genomics-based health revolution in India. It is the **first comprehensive pan-India initiative** providing a reference genome catalogue that represents India’s vast genetic diversity. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Gujarat has launched the “Creation of Reference Genome Database for Tribal Population in Gujarat,” a project that will sequence the genomes of 2,000 people from tribal communities in 17 districts. This is India’s **first state-level genomic project focusing solely on tribal groups**, aimed at better understanding their genetic health risks and improving precision healthcare. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Human Genome Project**, completed in **2003**, was the **first international scientific research project** **to sequence the entire human genome** and provide a complete reference for human genetic variation worldwide. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Gujarat government has launched India’s first tribal genome project to tackle inherited diseases.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
The term “GBU-57 Massive Ordnance Penetrator”,
recently in the news, refers to:

options.a:
The largest non-nuclear bomb in the US
inventory
options.b:
